sections.content:
**The highest-leverage AI habit is templating.** When you find a prompt that works well for a recurring task, save it as a template. Future you will be glad.

A prompt template is a proven prompt with placeholders for the parts that change each time:

```
Task: [WHAT YOU'RE WRITING]
Audience: [WHO WILL READ IT]
Key points to cover: [YOUR POINTS]
Tone: [professional / friendly / urgent]
Format: [bullets / paragraphs / sections]
Length: [word count or section count]
```

Build templates for tasks you do at least weekly:
- Weekly status updates
- Client check-in emails
- Performance feedback
- Slack announcements
- Proposal sections

Bonus technique: **chained workflow templates.** For multi-step tasks (like a full campaign brief), build a sequence of prompts — Prompt A produces output that becomes input for Prompt B. Document the chain so anyone on your team can run it.

A library of 10-15 well-tested templates can cut your daily AI time in half while improving consistency.

sections.bad: Writing a new prompt from scratch every time you need to write a weekly team update.
sections.good: Saving this template: 'Write a weekly team update for [TEAM NAME]. Audience: the team and our manager. Tone: direct and positive. Format: 3 sections — (1) Top wins this week (3 bullets), (2) In progress (2 bullets), (3) Blockers or needs (1-2 bullets). Keep total under 150 words. This week's content: [PASTE NOTES]' — and filling in the variables each Monday.
sections.explanation: The template took 10 minutes to write once. Now it takes 90 seconds to fill in each week. Over a year, that's hours saved — plus consistent quality. The team always knows what to expect from your updates.

sections.prompt: Write a reusable prompt template for a task you do regularly. Include at least 3 placeholders (in [CAPS] or {{brackets}}) for the parts that change each time.

rubric: A strong template: addresses a real recurring task, has at least 3 distinct placeholders, specifies format and tone clearly, is immediately usable by filling in the blanks, and produces a consistent output type each time
data.exampleAnswer: Write a follow-up email after [TYPE OF MEETING/CALL] with [PERSON'S NAME AND ROLE] from [COMPANY]. Goal: [DESIRED OUTCOME — schedule next step / send materials / confirm decision]. Key points to reference from the meeting: [YOUR BULLET POINTS]. Tone: [professional and warm / direct and brief / enthusiastic]. Include a clear single call to action at the end. Keep it under 150 words.

sections.prompt: You have a template that works well for one-on-one feedback. Your colleague asks you to share it. What's the best practice?

choices: Keep it private since it gives you a competitive advantage
Share the template and document any best practices for using it (what context to add, what outputs to expect)
Share the raw template with no explanation
Tell them to build their own
data.explanation: Sharing well-documented templates creates team-level leverage. Adding usage notes — what context makes it work best, what outputs to expect, how to iterate on results — means your colleague can use it effectively from day one. This is how AI productivity multiplies across teams instead of staying individual.
